content.text: I currently have seven active income
streams. Some are genuinely passive and
some require a lot more work to maintain
than you might think. In this video, I'm
going to rank all of my different income
streams across three different
categories so you can figure out which
one actually makes the most sense for
you to start and is the most realistic
for you based on where you are right
now. So, I'll rate each stream of income
by one, how easy it is to start. I'll
talk about how challenging it is to
actually set up and launch and what
skills you need to have in order to
succeed. Secondly, I'll give them each a
passive income score. So once you've got
that income stream up and running, how
much work is it actually going to be to
maintain? And lastly, I'll rate them
each on time to first income. So
realistically, how long is it going to
take from the day that you start until
you actually see money coming in? I'll
be dropping timestamps below this video
in case there's one specific side hustle
or business idea that you're
particularly interested in starting. But
if you're on the fence about anything or
you're just exploring some different
ways of making money online, you should
get cozy and maybe even grab a pen and
paper and jot down some notes and watch
this video all the way through to the
end. I'm going to be sharing some super
valuable information and I'm not
gatekeeping anything and I'm going to
tell you everything exactly how it is.
Now, before we dive into everything,
it's important that I emphasize that
even though I'm going to be talking
about the different ways that I make
money online, I am not a financial
adviser. So, this video is not meant to
be financial advice. I'm just sharing
what I've learned from my own experience
as an online entrepreneur. But,
everyone's situation is so different and
it's really important that you do your
own due diligence and make the best
decision for yourself and your finances.
Also, we're going to be talking about
this later in the video, but it's super
important to acknowledge upfront that
building each of these income streams
took a significant amount of effort.
These are not getrichqu schemes, and
there's no guarantee that you're going
to see the exact same results. My goal
with this video is to give you a
realistic look at what it takes to make
money online and how you can start
making passive income as a regular
person. Okay, now let's jump into the
first stream of income that I ever
started, Etsy. So, the first passive
income stream that I ever successfully
started was my Etsy shop. And I started
when I was a full-time middle school
teacher. As you probably know, teachers
don't get paid a ton, and I needed some
extra money, but I didn't have a bunch
of extra time to spend doing a typical
side hustle like Door Dash or Uber. But
I also didn't have any money set aside
to invest in starting a typical
business. So, I started selling Canva
templates on Etsy. It took about 8 days
from the day that I posted my first
product to get my first sale. And even
though those felt like the longest eight
days of my life, for the time to first
income category, I'm going to give the
stream of income five stars. It didn't
take that long for me to get my first
few sales, but it did take about 4
months for me to successfully scale my
shop to generate four figures a month.
Now, for the ease of starting category,
I'm going to give this one four out of
five stars. Like I mentioned, the whole
reason I even started this side hustle
in the first place was because it was
cheap to start. It was fully remote and
I could work as much or as little as I
wanted to with completely flexible
hours. That made it super accessible and
realistic for me to do even with a
full-time job. If it wasn't easy to get
started, I literally wouldn't have been
able to do it. But the reason I'm not
ranking it a full five out of five stars
is because there was definitely a
learning curve when it came to starting
my shop. I spent hours listening to
podcasts and watching YouTube videos
from different Etsy gurus online. And
there's a ton of not so great advice out
there when it comes to the business
strategy side of things. But before you
can even get to the business strategy
part of it all, you're going to have to
make digital products so you actually
have something to sell in the first
place. And you'll need to learn how to
design Etsy listing cover photos, which
are the photos that shoppers see on Etsy
when they're browsing different
products. This is extremely easy with
Canva. And if you're not super
techsavvy, I have a bunch of free Canva
tutorials on my channel. But in addition
to the basic graphic design skills,
there's also some more strategic and
technical stuff like keyword research,
search engine optimization, customer
retention, and pricing strategy, which
are important skills to learn as well,
but you don't have to have everything
figured out before you launch. These are
all things that you'll learn and
eventually master as you go. Back when I
had just started my shop, I would
probably rank the skills section at
three out of five stars. But since you
found my channel and you don't have to
navigate this all alone, I'll rank it a
four out of five stars for you. Now, for
the part that you're probably most
excited to hear, the passive income
score. My favorite thing about selling
digital products is that it made it
possible for me to start generating
passive income as a regular person.
There are a lot of misconceptions when
it comes to the concept of passive
income. So, real quick, let me explain
what it actually means. Passive income
is just money that you continue to make
for work that you've already done. Most
of us don't make our money this way,
right? So, we go to a job and we trade
our time for money. Whether you're
working an hourly job or you're paid a
salary, if you don't show up for work,
you don't get paid. But with any
business that has a passive income
business model, you don't have to trade
your time for money. You'll trade value
for money. So, you'll do the upfront
work to create or provide something
valuable, something that solves a
problem for people, and then you can
continue to sell that valuable thing
that you've created over and over. A
super common example of a type of
business with a passive income business
model is real estate. You buy an
apartment, you rent it out at a rate
that's higher than your monthly
mortgage, and then you pocket the
difference. But what if you don't have
tens of thousands of dollars sitting
around to drop on a down payment on a
piece of property? Or what if ethically
you just don't really like the idea of
being a landlord? Well, selling digital
products is a much more accessible,
realistic passive income business to
start. I don't know of any other
business where you can start for less
than 50 bucks. Spend as much time as you
have creating as many products as you
want to and then continue to generate
money from those products every single
day without having to continually trade
your time for money. I have products
that I listed on my shop that I posted
literally like 3 years ago and they're
still generating money for me every
single month. But it's important to
remember that every single passive
income stream, except for one strange
one that I'll mention later in this
video, but aside from that weird
exception, every single passive income
stream is going to require some level of
work to maintain. Just like with real
estate, right? So, when you buy and rent
out a house, you still have to handle
random things like maintenance requests.
So, if the air conditioning unit on your
property goes out, you're going to need
to fix that for your tenants. Or if
there's some random ladybug infestation
or something, it's your job to take care
of that. That is work, which is going to
take time. With selling digital
products, 99% of the work is done
upfront when you create and post your
product. But in order to maintain your
income stream, you're going to need to
do things like respond to messages from
customers. Some people might not be
techsavvy and they might not know how to
actually download their product. And
sometimes you might get messages from
shoppers who haven't purchased anything
yet who just have questions about your
product before they buy it. Stuff like
this is super infrequent, so I maybe
spend about 30 minutes a week handling
requests like this on my shop. But I
just want to be fully transparent in
case you're completely unfamiliar with
the concept of passive income. There is
going to be some sort of maintenance
involved. So for the passive income
score, I'm going to rank this one at a
four out of five stars. So I just gave
you the rundown on my first stream of
income, but that's just one of seven.
Once you factor in all the different
income streams that we'll get into later
in this video, when I first started
online entrepreneurship, my money
situation got really confusing really
fast. Before I had an automated system
to track everything, I genuinely had no
idea where I stood financially on any
given day. I'd look at my bank account
and think, "Okay, I have $400, but is
this actually mine to spend? Should I
put that aside for taxes, or is there a
brand deal payment that I'm expecting
that's late?" For my first year in
business, I was literally just holding
my breath every day and hoping for the
best. And that's what led me to Zero. If
your business or side hustle is anything
like mine, Zero is built specifically to
handle that. Zero is an accounting
software for small business owners and
it works exceptionally well for managing
the kind of situation that I was just
describing where you've got money coming
in from a bunch of different places and
on completely different sometimes
unpredictable schedules. The feature I
think is the most useful for me as an
online entrepreneur with multiple income
streams is the cash flow analysis
feature. It actually shows you
projections so you're not just seeing
where your money's at today, but you're
seeing what's actually coming so you can
plan ahead. So, if I know a brand deal
payment is landing in 2 weeks or if my
annual Canva subscription is coming out
next month, I can actually see that
adjustment in my anticipated balance.
Making that shift from constantly having
to check on upcoming payments and
charges and having to do the math in my
head to actually having a visual
dashboard and knowing what to expect
with my money has genuinely been a
gamecher for my business. And trust me,
if you're in the early stages of
building something, having that clarity
matters a lot. Huge shout out to Zero
for sponsoring this portion of today's
video. There's a special offer linked in
the description box below for 90% off
your first 6 months. It's definitely
worth checking that out, especially if
you don't have a solid, straightforward,
automated accounting system for your
business. That's not something that you
want to put off. Trust me, it's super
important and your future self will
thank you. Now, back to the list. Let's
move on to income stream number two. So,
my second stream of income is this
YouTube channel. But I actually didn't
monetize my YouTube channel in the
typical way that most new YouTubers do.
The way getting monetized on YouTube
works is once your channel hits 1,000
subscribers and 4,000 public watch
hours, you can apply for YouTube's
partner program. Once you're part of
that program, YouTube will pay you a
little bit over half of the money that
it generates from the automatic ads that
it places before, during, and after the
videos on your channel. But instead of
waiting for YouTube to approve me for
monetization, I decided to take matters
into my own hands and pursue brand
deals. Brand deals are basically when
brands compensate you directly for
mentioning their products or services in
your videos. Now, of course, the thing
about landing brand deals is that you
need a platform and an audience. I think
I had about 4,000 subscribers and 11
videos on my YouTube channel before I
landed my first brand deal. Because of
this, for the ease of starting category,
I'm going to rank this at a 2 out of
five stars. You don't need to be a viral
influencer by any means, but growing an
audience is definitely challenging. But
exactly how challenging it's going to be
is going to depend on the type of
content that you create. In my
experience, you really just need to have
the basic skills to get a decent video
uploaded. So, you'll need basic editing,
search engine optimization, and graphic
design skills. And of course, you're
going to want to be familiar with the
topic or topics that you're covering on
your channel. Picking a general niche
that aligns with the skills and
interests that you already have is going
to make this so much easier. For the
time to first income category, I'm going
to rate brand deals a two out of five
stars. Technically, when you have a
sponsored project, the payout process
isn't typically super long. Most times,
you'll get paid when the content is
approved, which from the time you first
land the brand deal to the time you
actually finish planning, filming,
editing, and re-editing, and then
submitting the project, it can be
anywhere from several weeks to several
months. It really just depends on the
scope of the project. Now, the reason
I'm rating this a two out of five stars
is because I'm factoring in all the time
that you're going to have to spend
making videos for free while you're
growing your audience from zero. Now,
for the passive income score, brand
deals get a zero out of five stars.
There is nothing passive about creating
brand sponsored content. You get paid an
agreed upon rate for an agreed upon
project or multiple projects. And once
that project is over, the payment is
given, and that's it. There's no passive
or residual income here. Now, moving on
to my next stream of income, which also
comes from this YouTube channel,
AdSense. This one is much more passive
than brand deals. Since I'm a part of
YouTube's partner program, my YouTube
channel is monetized through Google
AdSense. This means that anytime anyone
anywhere in the world watches one of my
YouTube videos and gets shown an ad or a
commercial, YouTube pays me a little
over half of the money that it makes
from showing that ad. That money or ad
revenue comes from the companies that
pay to have their ads shown on YouTube.
Now, technically, I don't actually get
paid for every single view, but I get
paid a set rate for every 1,000 views
that I get on each of my videos. This
pay rate is called RPM or revenue per
meal. So, if you're a growing content
creator working toward monetization or
even if you're just interested in making
YouTube videos, you might have already
done some research and discovered that
not all YouTubers have the same RPM. The
rate that we get paid on our videos
actually depends a lot on the video's
topic. Some niches like crypto, real
estate, marketing, and luxury cars pay a
much higher rate than others, like
makeup, gaming, and pop culture, for
example. But what you might not know is
that RPM doesn't actually just vary from
niche to niche. It can also vary from
video to video. And those rates can
fluctuate throughout the year. Because
ad revenue is highly volatile, it can
drop significantly during certain months
or when there's certain market changes.
It's never a good idea to rely solely on
AdSense as your only source of
stability. That's a huge part of the
reason why I'm making it a point to
diversify my income streams and not rely
solely on Google AdSense income. Now,
because RPM can vary so much from video
to video, this means that some videos
can generate way more money than others,
even if they get a lot less views. Let's
take a look at the behind-the-scenes
analytics of my YouTube channel. That
way, I can show you a real life example
of exactly how this works. All right, so
I've got the analytics pulled up for one
of the most popular videos on my
channel, which is called Digital
Marketing for Beginners. You can see
that I posted this video on May 7th,
2025. So, it's about a year ago from the
time that I'm filming this video. Since
this video was published, it's made
from ad revenue. And it's gotten a
little bit over $395,000
views. And we can actually look at the
RPM here. So overall, since the video's
been published, its average RPM is
Now, if we switch the time up here to
the last 28 days, we can see that in the
last 28 days, I've made $480.21
from this video. And down here you can
see that the video has gotten 23,800
views and the average RPM for the last
28 days has been $18.97.
So in the last month, the pay rate for
this video has gone up compared to the
average pay rate overall. So since the
RPM on this video is so high and people
are continuing to watch this video every
single day, even a year after I've
uploaded it, this video continues to
generate a good chunk of passive income
for me every single month. Now let's
take a look at another video. This video
is called How to Sell Canva Templates
for Beginners. I posted this video on
April 30th, 2025, which again was a
little bit over a year ago from the time
that I'm filming this. And right now,
this video has over a half a million
views. And even though this video has a
lot more views than the last video that
we looked at, the total lifetime AdSense
revenue for this video is $3,586.77.
That's just a little over half of the
revenue of the last video that we looked
at. So, even though this video has
gotten way more views overall, it's made
less money because of the RPM. Looking
[clears throat] at the lifetime
performance of this video, we can see
that the average RPM is $621.
That's a huge difference from that first
example that we looked at. So, for every
1,000 people who watch this YouTube
video, I get about $621 from YouTube.
And compared to the last example we
looked at where I make over $18 for
every 1,000 views, that is a huge

we can see that this video's gotten a

So, it's only made $178.85
in the last month. The fact that I

absolutely insane, and I feel so

YouTube. Most teachers are underpaid and
being able to teach here on YouTube
while also being able to pay down my

financial freedom and also
simultaneously building a community of
people who are also working toward that
same goal. It's not a privilege that I
take lightly at all. So, if you're
watching this right now, I just want to
say thank you so much for hanging out

making it possible for me to even make

support. Okay, now let me give you my
ratings for this income stream. For ease
of starting, I'll give it a two out of
five stars. Just like the brand deals
income stream I talked about earlier,
you do need to grow an audience to be
able to make money from Google AdSense.
You'll need 1,000 total subscribers on
your YouTube channel and 4,000 watch
hours within the last 365 days. It took
me about 10 weeks from the day that I
posted my first YouTube video to be able
to monetize [clears throat] my channel
this way. In the day that my channel got
monetized, I had 13 long- form videos
posted on my channel. This is actually
pretty quick relative to other YouTubers
that I've heard talk about their own
monetization journeys. Based on the
research that I've done, most new
creators don't actually reach 1,000
subscribers on their channels until
about 6 to 12 months after starting. And
that doesn't even account for the whole
watchtime hours requirement. Honestly
though, don't let those statistics
intimidate you because it's really only
as complicated as you make it. I didn't
know how to edit or anything before I

just sitting talking to the camera and

business. I didn't have any fancy
equipment or advanced skills of any
sort. So, my advice is to not over
complicate the video production side of
things. Just focus on making helpful
videos that people are looking for and
cover topics that you genuinely care
about. Now, because it takes most
creators several months or even years to
become eligible for monetization, I'm
going to rate time to first income at
two out of five stars. But there are
really straightforward strategies that
you can use to get monetized faster. I

searchable content, organizing my
content into playlists that would
encourage people to watch more, and
utilizing search engine optimization
when it came to planning my videos. I
have another video where I go way deeper

make sure to link it below for you in
case this particular income stream is
one that you're interested in starting.
Now, you might be surprised by this
because earlier I showed you a video
that I posted over a year ago that made
me almost $500 last month alone. But for
the passive income category, I'm giving
YouTube's AdSense program a four out of
five stars. Even though you technically
can make hundreds of dollars a month
from videos that are several years old,
that's not always guaranteed.
Realistically, not every video is going
to continue to get views every single
month. And since on YouTube, you get
paid per 1,000 views. Once a video stops
getting views, you're not going to
continue to make money from that video.
There's actually only one income stream
of mine that gets a perfect score in the
passive income category, and it's called
dividends. So, this is by far my
smallest stream of income, but it takes
absolutely zero work to maintain, and
it's the only income stream that gets a
full five out of five stars for the
passive income category. So, what are
dividends? Dividends are basically
recurring paychecks that some companies
pay you just for owning their stock. So,
they literally deposit money into your
account on a regular basis just for
being a shareholder. And obviously, not
just you specifically, but all of their
shareholders. Quick disclaimer before I
go any further. Investing in the stock
market involves real risk and you can
lose money. Everything that I'm sharing
here is just from my own experience and
it is not financial advice. The specific
companies that I'm going to be
mentioning are just popular examples and
they're not recommendations. I am not a
financial adviser, so please do your own
research and talk to a professional
before investing anything. I know that
might sound intimidating, but hear me
out because in my experience, this is
actually the most loweffort passive
income stream on this list. Before I
actually took the time to learn about
how the stock market worked, I had the
completely wrong idea. I thought that in
order to invest in the stock market, you
had to drop a huge chunk of money on a
stock and then just let it sit there for
years hoping that eventually the value
would go up. And only then could you
actually cash it out to see if it was
worth it. But here's what I didn't know.
You actually don't need hundreds of
dollars to start investing. You can
literally start with five bucks. If you
want to, you can literally invest one
time and just leave the money alone. And
if you choose stocks that pay dividends,
those companies will send you a cut of
their profits on a regular recurring
basis. Usually, it's quarterly, so every
3 months, but some companies even pay
dividends on a monthly basis. And that's
literal cash deposited into your
account. You don't have to sell anything
or do anything. The money just shows up
because you're a shareholder. That
sounds fake, right? Yeah, I thought so,
too. But the reason that some publicly
traded companies on the stock market pay
dividends is to encourage more people to
invest in their companies. Now, the
catch here is that you're probably not
going to get rich off dividends alone.
Companies that pay dividends pay all of
their shareholders a set percentage,
which varies from company to company.
And that means that you're going to
receive payments that are proportional
to the value of the stock that you own.
So, for example, one dividend paying
company that I've invested in is
Chevron. I'm not telling you what to do
with your money. This is just an example
of a popular dividend paying investment.
At the time that I'm filming this video,
their dividend yield is about 3.7%. So
whether I have $5, $500, or $5,000 worth
of Chevron stock, I'm going to receive
3.7% of the total value of my investment
in the form of recurring dividends. So
the bigger your investments, the bigger
your dividend payments are going to be.
But like I said, you can literally just

but their share price is really high,

For ease of starting, I'm going to rate

You can start with $5. It's super easy.

Now, for the passive income score, this

fully passive and there is zero
maintenance required. My next income
stream is technically two in one, and
that is my blog. A blog is just a niche
website where you post helpful articles
about a specific topic. It's sort of the
same idea as a YouTube channel, except
instead of videos, you're posting
articles. There's three main ways to
monetize a blog, and they're all
passive. The first is through ads. I
personally don't monetize my blogs this
way, but it is a very popular option for
bloggers. Once you hit certain traffic
requirements, so you have a certain
number of people coming to your blog and
reading your articles every month, you
can apply to be a part of an ad network.
That ad network will place certain
advertisements on your website. So
you'll see like advertisements pop up on
the sides next to your articles or even
pop-up ads that people have to click out
of before they can continue to read. And
similarly to the way that YouTube video
ads work, you as a blogger will get paid
a set rate depending on how many people
see those ads on your site. Now, the
next two methods of monetizing a blog
are both ways that I make money with my
blog. So, one is with digital products.
You can offer downloadable products on
your website that correlate with
whatever topic you talk about on your
website. So, if you've got a blog about
homeschooling, you could sell printable
lesson plans or activities or even
homeschool courses. Or if you have an
Etsy shop selling digital products, you
could start a website that's related to
that niche. And in addition to selling
your products on Etsy, you could also
post your digital products on your
website as well. And the final way to
monetize a blog is through affiliate
marketing. So in the articles on my
blog, if I mention a product or
software, I make sure to include my

clicks that link and purchases the
product that I recommend, the company
that they purchase from will share a
small percentage of that sale with me.
Now, of course, it doesn't cost my
readers any more money to purchase that
product or service. It's just the
company's way of thanking me for
bringing them a new customer. For ease
of starting, I'm going to give this one
a three out of five. Starting a blog
right now is way easier than it used to
be when I first started. There's so many
website building softwares that
integrate AI into the website building
process. So, nowadays, you don't really

You can literally just describe what you

technically it's pretty easy to actually
